title: Old Testament Types of Our Lady as Mediatrix and Co-Redemptrix — A Deeper Exegesis

types.title: The Protoevangelium
types.verse: Hebrew (Masoretic Text): *ואיבה אשית בינך ובין האשה ובין זרעך ובין זרעה הוא ישופך ראש ואתה תשופנו עקב* "And I will put enmities between thee and the woman, and thy seed and her seed: she [Vulgate: *ipsa*] shall crush thy head, and thou shalt lie in wait for her heel." (Genesis 3:15, Douay-Rheims)

types.title: The First Eve (Type by Inverse)
types.verse: "And Adam called his wife's name Eve [*Hawwah*, 'living']; because she was the mother of all the living." (Genesis 3:20, Douay)
types.body: Eve is the type by inverse. She is what Mary undoes by doing the opposite. The parallels are exact:

types.title: Rebekah, Mediatrix of the Blessing
types.verse: "And Isaac brought her into his mother Sara's tent, and took her to wife: and he loved her so much, that it moderated the sorrow which was occasioned by his mother's death." (Genesis 24:67) "And Rebecca said to Jacob her son: I heard thy father speaking with Esau thy brother... Now therefore, my son, follow my counsel..." (Genesis 27:6, 8)

types.title: Jacob's Ladder
types.verse: "And he saw in a dream a ladder standing upon the earth, and the top thereof touching heaven: the angels also of God ascending and descending by it... This is no other but the house of God, and the gate of heaven." (Genesis 28:12, 17)
types.body: Jacob's ladder is one of the most universal Marian types, found in every era of the tradition.

types.title: The Burning Bush
types.verse: "And the Lord appeared to him in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush: and he saw that the bush was on fire, and was not burnt." (Exodus 3:2)
types.body: The bush burns yet is not consumed. The presence of God dwells in created matter without destroying it. The type is exact for the Theotokos.

types.title: The Pillar of Cloud
types.verse: "And the Lord went before them to shew the way by day in a pillar of a cloud, and by night in a pillar of fire: that he might be the guide of their journey at both times." (Exodus 13:21)
types.body: The pillar of cloud and fire is the visible sign of God's presence guiding Israel through the wilderness. The cloud, in particular, is identified by the Fathers with the shekinah (the divine indwelling glory).

types.title: Miriam the Prophetess
types.verse: "So Mary [Miriam] the prophetess, the sister of Aaron, took a timbrel in her hand: and all the women went forth after her with timbrels and with dances: and she began the song to them, saying: Let us sing to the Lord, for he is gloriously magnified: the horse and his rider he hath thrown into the sea." (Exodus 15:20-21)
types.body: Miriam is a type by name and by function.

types.title: The Ark of the Covenant and the Mercy Seat
types.verse: "And they shall make an ark of setim wood... and thou shalt make a propitiatory of the purest gold... and thence will I give orders, and will speak to thee over the propitiatory, and from the midst of the two cherubim, which shall be upon the ark of the testimony, all things which I will command the children of Israel by thee." (Exodus 25:10, 17, 22)
types.body: The Ark of the Covenant is the densest and most extensively developed Marian type in the patristic tradition. Eight elements concur.

types.title: Aaron's Rod That Budded
types.verse: "He found that the rod of Aaron, for the house of Levi, was budded, and that the buds swelling it had bloomed blossoms, which spreading the leaves, were formed into almonds." (Numbers 17:8)
types.body: The dead rod of Aaron, placed in the tabernacle, miraculously buds and brings forth almonds without natural cause. The miraculous fruitfulness of what nature could not produce.

types.title: Deborah the Judge and Prophetess
types.verse: "Now Debbora, a prophetess, the wife of Lapidoth, judged the people at that time... And she said to him: Surely thou shalt go with me, but at this time the victory shall not be attributed to thee, because Sisara shall be delivered into the hand of a woman." (Judges 4:4, 9)
types.body: Deborah is a woman through whom God delivers Israel from a foreign oppressor. She is a judge (the only woman judge in Israel), a prophetess, and the strategic mind behind the victory. The Song of Deborah (Judges 5) is the second great song of Israel after the Song of Miriam.

types.title: Jael the Tent-Peg
types.verse: "And she went out to meet him, and said to him: Come in to me, my lord, come in, fear not... She took a nail of the tent, and taking also a hammer: and going in softly, and with silence, she put the nail upon the temples of his head, and striking it with the hammer, drove it through his brain fast into the ground." (Judges 4:18, 21)
types.body: Jael, a woman, crushes the head of Sisara, the tyrant general of Israel's oppressor. The verbal parallel to Genesis 3:15 is too close to miss: a woman, crushing the head of the enemy.

types.title: Gideon's Fleece
types.verse: "If thou wilt save Israel by my hand, as thou hast said, behold I will put this fleece of wool on the floor: if there be dew in the fleece only, and it be dry on all the ground beside, I shall know that by my hand, as thou hast said, thou wilt deliver Israel." (Judges 6:36-37)
types.body: Two miracles, in succession: dew on the fleece while the ground is dry, then the ground wet with dew while the fleece remains dry.

types.title: Hannah and Her Magnificat-Prefigurement
types.verse: "Hannah prayed, and said: My heart hath rejoiced in the Lord, and my horn is exalted in my God: my mouth is enlarged over my enemies: because I have joyed in thy salvation... He hath put down the mighty from their seat, and hath exalted the humble." (1 Samuel 2:1, 7-8)
types.body: Hannah's prayer in 1 Samuel 2 is the direct Old Testament template for Mary's Magnificat in Luke 1:46-55. The parallels are precise:

types.title: Bathsheba and the Davidic Queen Mother (Gebirah)
types.verse: "And Bathsheba came to king Solomon, to speak to him for Adonias: and the king arose to meet her, and bowed to her, and sat down upon his throne: and a throne was set for the king's mother, and she sat on his right hand. Then she said to him: I desire one small petition of thee; deny me not. And the king said to her: My mother, ask: for I must not turn away thy face." (1 Kings 2:19-20)
types.body: This is the single sharpest Mediatrix type in the entire Old Testament. The structural parallel is exact and detailed.

types.title: The Ark Comes to Jerusalem
types.body: See §8 above (the Ark of the Covenant). 2 Samuel 6 is the focal Old Testament text for the Marian Ark typology because of the verbal parallels with Luke 1:39-56.

types.title: Wisdom Personified
types.verse: "The Lord possessed me in the beginning of his ways, before he made any thing from the beginning. I was set up from eternity, and of old before the earth was made... When he prepared the heavens, I was present... I was with him forming all things." (Proverbs 8:22-23, 27, 30)
types.body: Wisdom in Proverbs 8 speaks in feminine voice and is personified as a preexistent figure present with God at creation. The principal Catholic reading of this passage is Christological: Wisdom is the Eternal Logos, the Word. This is the universal patristic understanding (Athanasius, Basil, Hilary all defend this against Arianism, which read Proverbs 8:22 as a proof that the Son was a creature).

types.title: The Valiant Woman (Mulier Fortis)
types.verse: "Who shall find a valiant woman? far and from the uttermost coasts is the price of her... Strength and beauty are her clothing, and she shall laugh in the latter day... Many daughters have gathered together riches: thou hast surpassed them all... Give her of the fruit of her hands: and let her works praise her in the gates." (Proverbs 31:10, 25, 29, 31)
types.body: The closing acrostic poem of Proverbs is read by the tradition both as a description of the ideal wife and, in sensus plenior, as a portrait of:

types.title: The Bride, the Enclosed Garden, the Closed Spring
types.verse: "Thou art all fair, O my love, and there is no spot in thee... A garden enclosed [*hortus conclusus*] is my sister, my spouse, a garden enclosed, a fountain sealed up." (Song of Songs 4:7, 4:12)
types.body: The Song of Songs is read by the Fathers and Doctors on three concentric levels:

types.title: Wisdom of Solomon 7-9, Sirach 24
types.verse: "I am the mother of fair love, and of fear, and of knowledge, and of holy hope... Come over to me, all ye that desire me, and be filled with my fruits. For my spirit is sweet above honey, and my inheritance above honey and the honeycomb." (Sirach 24:24, 26-27)
types.body: The deuterocanonical Wisdom literature continues and intensifies the personification of Wisdom from Proverbs 8. Sirach 24, in particular, has Wisdom speak in feminine voice and describe herself as universally fruitful, the source of every virtue, the inheritance of those who love her.

types.title: The Virgin Shall Conceive
types.verse: "Therefore the Lord himself shall give you a sign. Behold a virgin [*almah*; LXX *parthenos*] shall conceive, and bear a son, and his name shall be called Emmanuel." (Isaiah 7:14)
types.body: The most directly Marian text in the Old Testament after Genesis 3:15. The LXX parthenos (virgin) is preserved in Matthew 1:23 as the fulfillment text for the Annunciation. The dogmatic content (virginal conception) is more central than the Marian title.

types.title: The Closed Gate
types.verse: "And he brought me back to the way of the gate of the outward sanctuary, which looked towards the east: and it was shut. And the Lord said to me: This gate shall be shut; it shall not be opened, and no man shall pass through it: because the Lord the God of Israel hath entered in by it, and it shall be shut for the prince." (Ezekiel 44:1-3)
types.body: The closed eastern gate of the visionary temple. Through it the Lord enters; through it none other passes; it remains closed.

types.title: Daughter of Sion, the Lord in Thy Midst
types.verse: "Sing, O daughter of Sion: shout, O Israel: be glad and rejoice with all the heart, O daughter of Jerusalem... The Lord thy God is in the midst of thee, he is mighty, he will save: he will rejoice over thee with gladness, he will be silent in his love, he will be joyful over thee in praise." (Zephaniah 3:14, 17)
types.body: The Septuagint of Zephaniah 3:14 reads chaire sphodra, thugater Sion (Rejoice greatly, daughter of Sion). The angel Gabriel's greeting to Mary in Luke 1:28 is chaire kecharitomene. The verbal echo is deliberate: Mary is hailed as the Daughter of Sion personified, the place where God has come to dwell in the midst of His people.

types.title: Rejoice Greatly, O Daughter of Sion
types.verse: "Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Sion, shout for joy, O daughter of Jerusalem: BEHOLD THY KING will come to thee, the just and saviour: he is poor, and riding upon an ass, and upon a colt the foal of an ass." (Zechariah 9:9)
types.body: The same chaire greeting addressed to the Daughter of Sion, this time in the context of the coming of the messianic King. The verse is the direct Old Testament source for the Palm Sunday entry of Christ into Jerusalem (Matthew 21:5, John 12:15), but the chaire thugater Sion formula is the Old Testament root of the Lucan Annunciation greeting.

types.title: The Stone Cut Out Without Hands
types.verse: "Thus thou sawest, till a stone was cut out of a mountain without hands: and it struck the statue upon the feet thereof that were of iron and of clay, and broke them in pieces... but the stone that struck the statue, became a great mountain, and filled the whole earth." (Daniel 2:34-35)
types.body: The mountain from which the stone is cut without human agency is read by the Fathers as a type of Mary. The stone is Christ, cut from her without the action of man (the virginal conception). The mountain (Mary) is distinguished from the stone (Christ) but inseparable from it.

types.title: The Queen Who Risks Her Life to Intercede
types.verse: "All the king's servants, and all the provinces that are under his dominion, know, that whosoever, whether man or woman, cometh into the king's inner court, who is not called for, is immediately to be put to death without any delay: except the king shall hold out the golden sceptre to him... Now therefore, who knoweth whether thou art not therefore come to the kingdom, that thou mightest be ready in such a time as this?" (Esther 4:11, 14)
types.body: Esther, raised from among her condemned people to royal dignity, risks her life by entering the king's presence uninvited, to intercede for the salvation of her people. The king extends the golden sceptre; the intercession is accepted; the people are delivered.

types.title: The Woman Who Crushed the Tyrant's Head
types.verse: "And she struck twice upon his neck, and cut off his head, and took off his canopy from the pillars, and rolled away his headless body... Then Ozias the prince of the people of Israel, said to her: Blessed art thou, O daughter, by the Lord the most high God, above all women upon the earth." (Judith 13:10, 23)
types.body: Judith, alone, by her beauty, prudence, and faith, infiltrates the camp of Holofernes, the Assyrian general besieging Bethulia, and severs his head. The people are delivered without the loss of a single life.
